title: Turkish ebru marbling
subtitle: Floating pigments on a carrageenan size to produce patterned paper
excerpt: Ebru is the Ottoman Turkish marbling tradition: pigments mixed with ox-gall are dripped or brushed onto a thickened water surface, then drawn into patterns with a comb or stylus before a sheet is laid on top to lift the pattern. The result is a unique, unrepeatable sheet.
sourceNotes: Turkish ebru technique synthesised from established marbling practice. The carrageenan-size method is the standard modern Western adaptation of the traditional gum-tragacanth size used in Ottoman ebru.

glossaryTerms.definition: In marbling, the thickened water in the tray that holds the floating pigments. Made from carrageenan (a seaweed extract) dissolved in water. The viscosity of the size controls how the pigments spread and hold pattern.
A natural surfactant added to marbling pigments to reduce surface tension, allowing the paint to spread across the size rather than sitting in a ball. A single drop per colour is usually enough.

content.text: Ebru requires two preparation steps done the day before: a carrageenan

mixed and rested, and paper mordanted with alum solution. Both need time to settle. The marbling itself is fast, a tray of colour can be combed and printed in a few minutes, but the preparation is where most first-attempt failures occur.

attrs.title: Safety: alum mordant
attrs.body: Potash alum (potassium aluminium sulphate) is a mild irritant. Mix the alum mordant solution with gloves on, in a ventilated area. Do not let the solution dry on the skin. Alum is not acutely toxic at the concentrations used in marbling, but repeated skin contact should be avoided.

items.name: Marbling tray (slug: marbling-tray)
items.description: A shallow tray slightly larger than the paper you intend to marble. A clean plastic storage tub at least 50 mm deep works at home scale.

items.name: Carrageenan (slug: carrageenan)
items.description: Dissolved in cold water at 2, 3 g per litre and left to rest overnight. A cloudy, slightly viscous liquid.

items.name: Marbling acrylic (slug: marbling-acrylic-liquid)
items.description: Pre-thinned liquid acrylic in several colours. Each colour gets its own eyedropper.

items.name: Ox gall (slug: ox-gall)
items.description: Added drop by drop to each colour: 1 drop of ox gall per 10 ml of paint is a starting point.

items.name: Eyedropper (slug: eyedropper)
items.description: One per colour to avoid cross-contamination.

items.name: Ebru comb / stylus set (slug: ebru-comb)
items.description: Used to draw patterns through the floating colours before laying the paper.

items.name: Marbling paper, alum-mordanted (slug: marbling-paper-alum)
items.description: Paper brushed with alum solution (15 g alum per litre of water) and dried flat before use. The alum holds the pigment to the sheet.

items.name: Alum, potash (slug: alum-potash)
items.description: For mordanting the paper. Mix 15 g per litre of warm water, brush onto the paper surface, and allow to dry completely.

content.text: Dissolve 2, 3 g of carrageenan powder in 1 litre of cold water using a stick blender. The powder does not dissolve in hot water. Leave the size to rest for at least 8 hours (overnight is best) for bubbles to settle and viscosity to develop. The size should feel slightly slippery and hold a brief surface impression when you drag a finger across it.
Using the eyedropper, drop the first colour onto the size in a loose scatter. Each drop should spread to a disc 20, 40 mm across. If the drops sit in a ball without spreading, add more
to that colour. If the drops spread so far they thin out, reduce the ox gall. Add a second colour; the new drops will push the first outward. Continue building up colour before combing.

Draw the stylus end of the ebru comb through the colours in a straight line from one end of the tray to the other. Then draw the comb at right angles through those lines: this produces the classic wave or nonpareil pattern. For a bouquet or peacock feather, use the stylus alone to draw small S-curves through the combed lines. Work quickly, the colours are live for 2, 3 minutes before they begin to dry at the edges.

Hold the mordanted paper by its two long edges, bowing it gently downward. Lower the centre of the sheet onto the size first, then release the edges to lie flat. Press no part of the sheet, simply lay it on. Wait 15, 20 seconds. Lift the paper by one corner in a smooth diagonal motion, draining the size back into the tray. Rinse the printed side immediately under a gentle stream of water to remove the size, which would dry sticky. Lay flat to dry.
